data.title: Scotiabank to acquire MapleMark, accessing FDIC insurance for clients
data.description:
The Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), known as Scotiabank, agreed to acquire Maple Financial Holdings, the parent of MapleMark Bank, giving it FDIC deposit insurance for its clients, the company said Friday. Moreover, the transaction will expand its presence in the U.S.

Gaining Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. protection is important for Scotiabank's mortgage capital markets business and its deposit growth strategy, Travis Machen, CEO and group head of Scotiabank's Global Banking and Markets, said. "MapleMark Bank is a well-run bank primarily operating in Dallas, Texas, and further supports our strategic focus within the North American corridor," he said.

The acquisition isn't expected to have a material impact on Scotiabank's earnings or CET1 ratio, it said.

Scotiabank (BNS) stock slipped 0.3% in morning trading.

data.title: PagerDuty outlines FY2027 EPS of $1.27 to $1.32 while expanding Operations Cloud usage-based pricing
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PagerDuty (PD) Q1 FY2027

Management View

  • "In Q1, PagerDuty delivered results that exceeded the top end of guidance for both revenue and non-GAAP operating margin." (Executive Chair of the Board Jennifer Tejada)

  • "Quarterly revenue was $121 million, up 1% year-over-year, and annual recurring revenue was $496 million, flat year-over-year." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • "We grew non-GAAP operating margin to 25% through consistent discipline, structural efficiency initiatives and AI adoption." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • "Usage-based products, which include AIOps, PagerDuty Advance and Operations Cloud, now account for nearly 10% of our total ARR." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • "The ARR of customers on this model nearly doubled from Q4 to Q1." (CFO & Principal Financial Officer Howard Wilson)

  • "After 10 years as CEO, I've transitioned to Executive Chair." (Executive Chair Tejada) "I'm pleased to introduce PagerDuty's new CEO, John DiLullo." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • "In the near term, my priority is simple: listen, learn and engage." (CEO & Director John DiLullo)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter fiscal 2027, we expect revenue in the range of $122 million to $124 million" and "net income per diluted share attributable to PagerDuty, Inc. in the range of $0.29 to $0.31." (CFO Wilson)

  • "This implies an operating margin of 22% to 23%." (CFO Wilson)

  • "For the full fiscal year 2027, we expect revenue in the range of $488.5 million to $496.5 million" and "net income per diluted share attributable to PagerDuty, Inc. in the range of $1.27 to $1.32." (CFO Wilson)

  • "This is the same range as previously provided." (CFO Wilson) "[EPS is] an increase based on the reduced share count as a result of the completion of the buyback program." (CFO Wilson)

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter of FY '27... Revenue for the quarter was $121 million" and "Q1 gross margin was 86%" while "operating income was $30 million or 25% of revenue." (CFO Wilson)

  • "GAAP net income was $10.2 million, our fourth consecutive quarter of GAAP profitability." (CFO Wilson)

  • "Dollar-based net retention was 97%." (CFO Wilson) "We expect our continued customer success and renewal initiatives, along with our Operations Cloud pricing to result in stabilization of DBNR and for it to gradually increase throughout the year." (CFO Wilson)

  • "Cash from operations was $44 million" and "free cash flow was $41 million." (CFO Wilson)

  • "We ended the quarter with $444 million in cash, cash equivalents and investments." (CFO Wilson) "At the end of Q1, total RPO was approximately $441 million." (CFO Wilson)

  • "During the quarter, we repurchased 8.5 million shares for $63 million and completed the authorized $200 million share repurchase program." (CFO Wilson) "Today, we have announced our latest $100 million share repurchase program." (CFO Wilson)

Q&A

  • Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: "the net retention rate did take a step down"—what drives confidence in stabilization? Executive Chair Tejada: "we have really started to see good progress with our early cohort in the pricing transition" and "we're seeing very strong demand signals" including "our fifth consecutive quarter of over 600 new customer logos." CFO Wilson: "we monitor closely the growth in usage on our platform" and "we saw from Q4 to Q1, nearly doubling the ARR of customers who were on that model."

  • Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: what are CEO priorities? CEO DiLullo: "It's really early days for me" and "AI continues to accelerate the creation of growth" and "more software, more dependencies, more automation means more inference, more compute" which "lead[s] to just greater need for resilience and orchestration and real-time operational response."

  • Jonathan Ngo, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division: balancing "heavy R&D" for agents with margin expansion? Executive Chair Tejada: "We have been building AI into the platform for several years" and "we're able to do that on a lower cost basis than we have in the past." CFO Wilson: "we've always taken a balanced view on capital allocation" and "the one strength that we have... is our gross margins."

  • Andrew Sherman, TD Cowen, Research Division: where can consumption ARR mix go by year-end? CFO Wilson: "We haven't put a specific number out there" and "our expectation is that we will make meaningful progress with this each period as we go through the year."

  • Andrew Sherman, TD Cowen, Research Division: update to AI-native ARR mix? Executive Chair Tejada: "some of these native AI companies are scaling really fast" and "we're seeing the appetite for resilience at scale coming much earlier" while "the most demanding developers are choosing the best-in-breed offering early" through "our PLG motion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "the net retention rate did take a step down" and questions on margin durability signaled slightly negative to neutral analyst tone, centered on retention mechanics, pricing migration pacing, and investment needs. (Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley, Research Division; Jonathan Ngo, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division; Andrew Sherman, TD Cowen, Research Division)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A, leaning on confidence markers including "We are confident" (Executive Chair Tejada) and emphasizing early traction: "The ARR of customers on this model nearly doubled" (CFO Wilson).

  • Versus last quarter, the call added leadership-transition uncertainty but maintained similar messaging on consumption pricing and AI as a driver; Q&A focus remained anchored on retention and the pricing shift (Q4: "seat-based compression"; Q1: "mitigate some of the pressure around gross retention because of seat-based compression"). (Executive Chair Tejada; CFO Wilson)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • "After 10 years as CEO, I've transitioned to Executive Chair" marked a leadership change versus Q4, when Tejada spoke as "Chair of the Board & CEO." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • Q1 emphasized Operations Cloud early traction in quantified terms: "The ARR of customers on this model nearly doubled from Q4 to Q1," versus Q4 framing that "we anticipate by the end of this fiscal year that a meaningful portion of our ARR will be under the new licensing model" without a disclosed migration metric. (CFO Wilson)

  • Guidance posture stayed flat on FY2027 revenue: Q1 said "This is the same range as previously provided," while raising EPS range due to "reduced share count" after completing the buyback program and launching a new authorization. (CFO Wilson)

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4 go-to-market execution and pricing receptivity to Q1 scrutiny on "net retention" stabilization and the practical pace of consumption expansion within ARR. (Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley, Research Division; Andrew Sherman, TD Cowen, Research Division)

Risks and Concerns

  • "not every customer in our base is exactly the same" and management described parallel pressures from customers "in the transitional phase" alongside expansion opportunities tied to usage-based pricing. (CFO Wilson)

  • "Our customer base is also in transition" and "we have some segments of the customer base that are under more financial pressure than others, including like midsized SaaS." (Executive Chair Tejada)

  • "Q1 free cash flow was elevated primarily due to overperformance on collections, which we expect to normalize in Q2" and "part of the Q1 overperformance was due to marketing program spend, which we expect to deploy in Q2." (CFO Wilson)

Final Takeaway

PagerDuty’s management positioned the quarter around a CEO transition, a usage-based business model shift, and sustained margin discipline, highlighting that Operations Cloud pricing delivered early traction (including nearly doubled ARR among customers on the model from Q4 to Q1) while reiterating flat full-year revenue guidance and lifting full-year EPS guidance tied to share repurchases and a reduced share count.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: "AI that predicts clinical outcomes?" Tempus AI in focus after ASCO data reveal
data.description:
Tempus AI (TEM) stock immediately jumped over ~3% to ~$53.15 before pulling back on Friday after unveiling new AI-driven precision medicine data at the 2026 ASCO Annual Meeting.

The healthcare technology company said its latest multimodal foundation model was trained on 2.5M longitudinal patient records, including 250M clinical note pages, 450K medical images, and 500K genomic and transcriptomic sequences.

The management believes the model can help accelerate drug development, improve clinical trial design, and identify high-risk cancer patients earlier.

In a study involving EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer patients treated with osimertinib, the model showed strong predictive accuracy with a C-index of 0.802 and significant survival stratification across patient groups.

CEO Eric Lefkofsky said the company’s AI models are already outperforming smaller specialized models in predicting clinical outcomes.

Moreover, over the last five days, Tempus AI (TEM) has climbed ~10.68%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) return of 0.66%.

data.title: Consumer spending modest, households and businesses cautious - Anna Paulson
data.description:
Growth and consumer spending are modest, and households and businesses are cautious, according to Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Anna Paulson.

Paulson was speaking on the economic outlook before the Chamber of Commerce Southern New Jersey.

"Consumers are continuing to spend, although at a slower pace. In the first quarter, consumption grew at an annual rate of 1.4%, and that's down from 2.1% last year," she said.

"We continue to hear about consumers trading down from premium brands to cheaper alternatives, and some families are relying more on credit cards and other debt to maintain spending," said Paulson.

"While many families are feeling stretched by inflation, there are few signs that consumers are pulling back sharply in the aggregate," said the Federal Open Market Committee's voting member.

However, many consumers express pessimism about the economy and about their prospects.

Regarding what AI will mean for jobs, Paulson said there's growing pressure in corporate boardrooms to translate AI efficiencies into cost savings, including through layoffs. But not all their contacts share that view.

"Many expect AI to slow hiring rather than to trigger job cuts," she said.

"My base case is that the labor market will remain stable and that we will end the year with an unemployment rate in the range that has prevailed over the last few years," said the Philadelphia Fed president.

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: These 10 large-cap U.S. stocks rank among the market's cheapest names
data.description:
A screen of U.S. stocks with market capitalizations above $10B highlights Pfizer (PFE), Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA), and Edison International (EIX) among the market's most attractively valued companies relative to their sector peers.

Seeking Alpha's valuation grade compares how expensive or cheap a stock is relative to others in its sector. It is based on a combination of valuation metrics such as P/E, PEG, EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA, EV/EBIT, Price/Sales, Price/Book, Price/Cash Flow, and dividend yield, using both current and forward estimates.

Most cheap U.S. stocks by valuation grade (market cap $10B and above):

  • Pfizer (PFE): Valuation Grade A
  • Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA): Valuation Grade A
  • Edison International (EIX): Valuation Grade A
  • Dow Inc. (DOW): Valuation Grade A
  • HP Inc. (HPQ): Valuation Grade A
  • Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation (FMCC): Valuation Grade A
  • Southwest Airlines (LUV): Valuation Grade A
  • Charter Communications, Inc. (CHTR): Valuation Grade A
  • General Mills (GIS): Valuation Grade A
  • Blue Owl Capital (OWL): Valuation Grade A

data.title: Chicago PMI surges past consensus in May
data.description: The Chicago PMI jumped to 62.7 in May from 49.2 in March, beating the 50.3 consensus, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management on Friday.

data.title: The AI boom is creating jobs, not destroying them, Apollo says
data.description:
Artificial intelligence is fueling job creation rather than eliminating positions, according to Apollo Chief Economist Torsten Slok, who argues that current labor market data show no sign of AI-driven employment losses.

Slok pointed to weekly ADP employment figures, which have strengthened steadily in recent months, with hiring momentum accelerating into the spring. The data suggest that businesses continue to add workers even as investments in artificial intelligence technologies surge across industries. 

Instead of replacing large numbers of employees, companies are actively recruiting specialists to deploy and manage AI systems. Demand for AI implementation experts has increased alongside a massive wave of spending on data centers, cloud infrastructure, and computing capacity. That buildout is also contributing to higher wages for skilled technology workers while pushing up costs for semiconductors, equipment, and energy. 

The trend reflects what economists call the Jevons Paradox, where greater efficiency lowers costs and ultimately drives higher consumption rather than reducing demand. In the case of AI, cheaper and more accessible technology is encouraging broader adoption, leading to additional investment, hiring, and economic activity. 

As a result, Slok believes the labor market may be stronger than widely anticipated. He said May nonfarm payrolls could come in well above the consensus forecast of 95,000, highlighting the continued employment boost from the ongoing AI spending boom. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: SpaceX reportedly lowers IPO valuation target to at least $1.8T; Musk denies it
data.description:
SpaceX (SPCX) is targeting a valuation of at least $1.8T for its upcoming IPO, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The target is below the more than $2T valuation reported earlier in April, reflecting feedback from advisers and prospective investors during the IPO planning process, the people said. Valuation and deal size remain subject to change as marketing progresses.

The company is seeking to raise as much as $75B, which would make the offering the largest IPO on record, according to people familiar with the plans.

However, SpaceX CEO Elon Musk denied the lower valuation in a post on X, writing "false." 

Formal marketing of the IPO could begin as early as June 4, with pricing potentially taking place around June 11, Bloomberg News previously reported. However, the timetable could shift by several days.

The IPO is being led by Goldman Sachs (GS), Morgan Stanley (MS), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM), alongside 18 additional underwriting banks.

SpaceX, formally known as Space Exploration Technologies Corp., plans to list on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas under the ticker symbol SPCX.

It generated $18.7B in revenue in 2025, and its IPO materials highlight the company's transformation from a space and satellite communications provider into a broader AI and infrastructure platform targeting an estimated $28.5T total addressable market.

(This story has been updated to include Musk's response.)

data.title: Gap, American Eagle stumble as fashion miscues dress down Q1 results
data.description:
Gap (GAP) shares continue to unravel and are expected to snap a six-day winning streak for the stock, as soft comparable store sales at its Old Navy banner and lowered guidance overshadowed impressive results at Gap stores.

Comparable store sales at Old Navy and Banana Republic were less than half of what was expected, while the struggling Athleta brand realized a further deterioration in sales.

Although the results rattled investors, the sales miss can largely be blamed on a “self-inflicted fashion miss” rather than a cash-strapped consumer or broader macroeconomic weakness.

"From what we can see today, the consumer remains resilient, and while we continue to monitor their behavior, at this time, our outlook does not assume any meaningful shift over the balance of the year," Gap CFO Katrina O'Connell said on the company’s earnings call Thursday.

And Wall Street analysts largely agree.

“We think the company was smart to come out and diagnose the Q1 Old Navy shortfall as an internal misstep on fashion—which it can fix—instead of a macro issue or leaving investors wondering if strong [same store sales] at all 3 off-pricers are taking outsized bites out of Old Navy sales. Both would be harder to reverse than a self-inflicted fashion miss,” said Evercore ISI analyst Michael Binetti.

Although Binetti downgraded Gap Inc. (GAP) to In Line from Outperform, the action reflects a “complicated” FY26 outlook due to Old Navy’s deceleration rather than a loss of confidence in the parent company.

Agrees BofA Securities’ Lorraine Hutchinson. “The weakness was driven by a seasonal assortment (dresses, shorts, and swim) that didn’t resonate.” 

The mood at Gap (GAP) mirrors that at peer American Eagle (AEO) where the company’s management also views headwinds undermining sales as temporary, especially as the retailer enters the critical back-to-school season.

“We think gas prices hopefully will start settling down very shortly, and with the current affairs hopefully will come to some type of finish; hopefully it will be a very good finish for the world. And so we're very optimistic on that," American Eagle CEO Jay Schottenstein said on the company’s earnings call.
